coredata.dc:title: Efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production using carbon dots-infused g-C3N4 nanoreactors synthesized via one-step template-free thermal polymerization
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xinhai
Chen, Zhouze
Shen, Yu
Qin, Haoyuan
Yuan, Hao
Lu, Jialin
Guo, Feng
Li, Chunsheng
Shi, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based materials present promising prospects as photocatalysts for water splitting in hydrogen (H2) production, yet the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) is typically intricate and involves multiple steps, limiting its practical utility. In a groundbreaking approach, this study achieves the uniform and secure embedding of CDs onto the surface of porous graphitic carbon nitride vesicles (PCNVs) through one-step template-free thermal polymerization. This results in the creation of CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors designed for efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 precipitation under simulated/real sunlight irradiation. Notably, the optimal prepared CDs/PCNVs-3 sample exhibited a H2 production rate of 25.86 mmol h−1 g−1, surpassing that of pure PCNVs by 2.2 times. The heightened photocatalytic activity primarily stems from the following reason: (i) CDs can effectively improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs on the surface of the photocatalysts; (ii) the unique porous cavity structure of PCNVs can improve light absorption through the multiple reflections and scattering; (iii) the coupling of the photothermal conversion performance of CDs and the thermal insulation of PCNVs photo-thermal nanoreactors improves the photocatalytic activity from the perspective of photo-thermal assistance. Considering the simplicity and economic viability of current synthesis method for modifying CDs on the surface of photocatalysts, it offers a promising strategy for developing highly efficient CDs-based photothermal-assisted composite photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production using carbon dots-infused g-C3N4 nanoreactors synthesized via one-step template-free thermal polymerization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based materials present promising prospects as photocatalysts for water splitting in hydrogen (H2) production, yet the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) is typically intricate and involves multiple steps, limiting its practical utility. In a groundbreaking approach, this study achieves the uniform and secure embedding of CDs onto the surface of porous graphitic carbon nitride vesicles (PCNVs) through one-step template-free thermal polymerization. This results in the creation of CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors designed for efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 precipitation under simulated/real sunlight irradiation. Notably, the optimal prepared CDs/PCNVs-3 sample exhibited a H2 production rate of 25.86 mmol h−1 g−1, surpassing that of pure PCNVs by 2.2 times. The heightened photocatalytic activity primarily stems from the following reason: (i) CDs can effectively improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs on the surface of the photocatalysts; (ii) the unique porous cavity structure of PCNVs can improve light absorption through the multiple reflections and scattering; (iii) the coupling of the photothermal conversion performance of CDs and the thermal insulation of PCNVs photo-thermal nanoreactors improves the photocatalytic activity from the perspective of photo-thermal assistance. Considering the simplicity and economic viability of current synthesis method for modifying CDs on the surface of photocatalysts, it offers a promising strategy for developing highly efficient CDs-based photothermal-assisted composite photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy stands out as an optimal solution for addressing energy scarcity and mitigating air pollution, representing a pivotal strategy for China to actualize its “dual-carbon policy” [1–5]. Nevertheless, the widespread application of hydrogen energy faces significant challenges due to inherent limitations, including the complexities associated with hydrogen storage and transportation, the elevated energy consumption inherent in traditional water electrolysis [6–8]. Photocatalytic technology emerges as a promising avenue to overcome these drawbacks, transcending the conventional H2 production methods reliant on finite fossil fuels (coal and oil), which facilitates the eco-friendly, energy-efficient, user-friendly, and low-pollution water splitting into H2 [9–12]. Despite significant advancements in semiconductor photocatalyst research, existing materials encounter a multitude of challenges [13–15], including rapid electron-hole pairs’ recombination and limited light absorption ranges, which substantially hindered the efficacy of photocatalytic H2 production and impeded practical applications. Consequently, there is an urgent need to optimize photocatalyst activity through diverse strategies aimed at enhancing light absorption, promoting efficient charge separation and migration, and facilitating surface redox reactions, which still remains a major challenge in the photocatalytic field [16–19].

A highly promising approach to achieving efficient photocatalytic activity involves tuning the interfaces and surface engineering of semiconductors. In this regard, the tunable cavities and internal void spaces within hollow shell structures serve as advantageous nanoreactors, can effectively improve the photocatalytic performance [20], which can be attributed to several benefits: (i) the voids enable multiple reflections and scattering of light, augmenting sunlight capture and utilization [21–24]; (ii) thinner hollow shell assembly effectively reduces the transmission distance of light-generated charges [25,26]; (iii) confining the primary sites of reactions within the nanospace generates a photothermal effect through the shell material, creating a thermal insulation effect within the cavity [2,27], and this, in turn, elevates the local temperature of the catalyst, contributing to the migration and transport of photo-induced carriers [28]. Building upon our prior research, we successfully synthesized porous g-C3N4 vesicles (PCNVs) in our previous study through template-free self-assembly, whose structures exhibit unique characteristics and desirable tunable physicochemical properties, holding significant promise for the design of PCNVs-based nanoreactors [29]. For instance, Lu et al. engineered a Co3O4/PCNVs S-scheme heterojunction nanoreactor through the deposition of Co3O4 nanoparticles onto the surface of PCNVs via hydrothermal approach, which demonstrated significant efficacy in achieving highly efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production [30]. Guo’s group engineered Ag2S/PCNVs nanoreactors through the surface encapsulation of porous graphite carbon nitride nanovesicles (PCNVs) with silver sulfide quantum dots (Ag2S QDs). This approach leveraged the synergistic interplay between the photothermal conversion capabilities inherent in the plasmonic resonance effect of the Ag2S QDs and the thermal insulation characteristics of the PCNVs photo-thermal nanoreactors, thereby culminating in a substantial enhancement of photothermal-assisted activity [31]. Despite these confirmations of the advantages associated with vesicle-structured nanoreactors, their exploration and utilization in the realm of photothermal-assisted photocatalysis remain in their nascent stages. Substantial challenges persist, particularly in the production processes, involving intricate steps of processing and purification that contribute to elevated production costs. Consequently, there exists an exigent requirement to devise a facile and environmentally sustainable methodology for fabricating photothermal-assisted photocatalytic nanoreactors conducive to efficient solar photocatalytic water splitting.

Significantly, the utilization of carbon dots (CDs), acknowledged as the predominant photothermal materials, incorporated into a g-C3N4 support matrix, stands out as one of the most potent strategies for the enhancement of photothermal-assisted photocatalysis [32–37]. CDs serve as a photothermal material, have the capability to elevate the surface temperature of photocatalysts, which result in expediting the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the distinctive up-conversion luminescence properties exhibited by CDs contribute to broadening the spectrum of solar light absorption, thereby augmenting photocatalytic efficiency [38–42]. Thirdly, the remarkable induced electron transfer property of CDs contributes to the effective separation of g-C3N4 photogenerated hole-electron pairs [43–46]. However, in the majority of current literature reports, CDs/g-C3N4 composites are typically synthesized by first preparing the CDs and then subsequently compositing them with CN through various methods, which exhibit complex and time-consuming features, and it can also lead to a weak and uneven interfacial contact between CDs and g-C3N4. For instance, Tang et al. synthesized CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts through the pyrolysis of ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), melamine calcination, bulk g-C3N4 stripping, and hydrothermal treatment [47]. Similarly, Liang et al. prepared CDs/g-C3N4 using a process that involved urea pyrolysis, g-C3N4 acid treatment, CDs electrochemical synthesis, and the physical mixing of CDs and g-C3N4 photocatalysts [38]. Subsequent investigations inspired us to delve into the simpler synthesis of CDs embedded within PCNVs, aiming at achieving efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water splitting without compromising the integrity of the vesicle-structured nanoreactor.

In this work, CDs were uniformly and firmly embedded on the surface of g-C3N4 photocatalysts through one-step template-free self-assembly, resulting in the formation of a CDs/PCNVs nanoreactor designed for efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production. This nanoreactor system marks the first attempt at one-step synthesis of CDs, and it delves into the combined effects of the photothermal properties of CDs and the insulating effects of PCNVs. Comprehensive analyses of the physical and chemical properties of the synthesized composites were conducted using various analytical techniques. Furthermore, the impact of increasing the temperature of the reaction solution on photocatalyst activity was investigated by controlling the experimental temperature. Simultaneously, infrared thermograms were employed to study temperature changes inside and outside the CDs/PCNVs nanoreactor, shedding light on the unique role of the structure and the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic mechanism.

Phloxine B (C20H2Br4Cl4Na2O5), melamine, cyanuric acid, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and triethanolamine (TEOA) are purchased from Macklin Reagents Co., Ltd. All the chemicals in the experiment were of analytical grade without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors
ce:section.ce:para: The porous g-C3N4 vesicle nanoreactors (PCNVs) were prepared by a facile thermal co-polymerization method according to our previous research [29]. Then, the synthesis process of CDs/PCNVs was similar to that of PCNVs, except that a certain amount of Phloxine B was added prior to the process of calcination. The samples were named as CDs/PCNVs-1, CDs/PCNVs-3, CDs/PCNVs-5 and CDs/PCNVs-7 according to the amounts of Phloxine B added (1 mg, 3 mg, 5 mg and 7 mg). In addition, for the sake of comparison, the bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by a one-step calcination strategy of melamine (550 ℃, 2.3 ℃/min, 4 h) and named as BCN.

Photocatalytic H2 production reaction, which consists of a top irradiation vessel connected to a glass gas circulation system (CEL-PAEM-D8, Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co., Ltd.) with N2 as the carrier gas. In general, 25 mg sample was dispersed in a 45 mL aqueous solution containing 1 wt% Pt served as co-catalyst and 5 mL Triethanolamine (TEOA) as sacrificial reagent. The escaping gas for calculating H2 production and H2 production rate was analyzed by gas chromatography (Agilent 7890B GC), and the 300 W Xenon lamp was used as an irradiated light source. The initial temperature of the water was set at about 12 ℃ by evacuating the equipment, and the temperature change of the powder of the synthesized sample in water under light for 2 h was recorded using an infrared thermography camera (FLIRR, C3-X) to investigate the effect of photo-thermal effect on photocatalytic H2 production. The effect of temperature on photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production from the composites was quantified by controlling the water temperature of the device (5, 15 and 25 °C) through a recirculating condensate device in comparison with the room temperature (RT). The temperature changes of the solid powder of the synthesized samples were recorded under light illumination for 8 min and natural cooling for 8 min to study the insulating properties of the prepared nanoreactors.

The temperature changes of the solid powder of the synthesized samples were recorded under 8 min of light and 8 min of natural cooling to investigate the insulation performance of as-prepared samples. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of the photocatalyst was measured by 420, 650 and 800 nm band-pass filter with the same reaction conditions, and AQE could be calculated by follow equation (Eq. (1):

In addition, Photothermal conversion efficiency calculations, photochemical tests and characterizations are listed in the Supporting Information .

The synthesis methods for CDs are typically intricate, and the interfacial contacts between CDs and g-C3N4 are often weak and inhomogeneous. Obviously, in order to enable the practical applications of CDs-modified g-C3N4 materials, a novel and straightforward synthesis technique is highly desired while simultaneously achieving uniform and robust interface contacts. Herein, Fig. 1 illustrates a template-free self-assembly synthesis process for the production of CDs/PCNVs, in which CDs are systematically and securely embedded into the surface of porous g-C3N4 vesicle nanoreactors through a simple thermal polymerization. Initially, in the early stage, after the dissolution process, the oxygenated hydroxyl groups of cyanuric acid can form strong hydrogen bonds with the amino groups of melamine in dimethyl sulfoxide solution [48], which saturates in DMSO solvent to form a complete self-assembly, constituting supramolecular aggregates [49]. Due to the acidic nature of the enol groups in Phloxine B and the basic nature of the amino groups at the edges of the supramolecular aggregates, Phloxine B tightly attaches to the surface of the supramolecular aggregates (Fig. S1). The loss of material mass with the thermal polymerization process leads to large voids in the precursor, formed during pyrolysis due to structure collapse and gas shock [50], thereby forming the PCNVs substrate material. Simultaneously, the high-temperature carbonization of Phloxine B allows the in-situ formation of CDs, ensuring their uniform and secure modification on the surface of PCNVs.

To validate the viability of this synthesis approach, the surface morphology of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs samples was first characterized by employing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As can be seen from the Fig. 2 a and b, the prepared PCNVs have a distinctive hollow vesicle structure, and CDs/PCNVs sample obtained through this synthesis method and exhibits similar morphological features, suggesting that the incorporation of Phloxine B during the process of synthesis and the subsequent carbonization has not been significantly disrupted the structure of the hollow vesicle structure. However, for the CDs/PCNVs samples, no CDs were observed in Fig. 2c, possibly due to their extremely small particle size [51]. From the TEM image in Fig. 2d, it was further confirmed that the PCNVs possess cavity structures with a diameter of about 200–300 nm, functioning as photocatalytic nanoreactors allowing multiple reflections of light in the cavity [49]. For the CDs/PCNVs sample (Fig. 2e), the CDs can be embedded and confined in-situ in the nanoreactors by a thermal polymerization treatment with Phloxine B, which helps to obtain a large specific surface area and more exposure of active sites [52]. Upon closer inspection in the enlarged TEM image (Fig. 2f), the PCNVs surface revealed numerous black dots within the red circles, which indicated well-dispersed CDs in the PCNVs matrix without evident clusters. Additionally, CDs with an average size of about 5 nm can be clearly seen in Fig. 2g, uniformly dispersed on the surface of PCNVs, aligning with previous reports [53]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of Fig. 1f reveals a d-spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (001) crystalline surface of the carbon [54,55], providing preliminary confirmation of CDs introduction into the CDs/PCNVs composite system. In addition, elemental mapping images (Fig. 2i) confirmed the presence of the elements C, N and O. Obviously, the above results strongly confirm the preliminary success of CDs/PCNVs. Crucially, the close contact and uniform distribution of CDs on PCNVs, facilitated by CDs modification, lay the foundation for effective carrier migration at the interface, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity [56,57].

To scrutinize the crystal structure of the as-prepared photocatalysts, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs composites with various mass ratios were meticulously analyzed. In Fig. 3 a, for pristine PCNVs, the discernible characteristic peaks at approximately 12.9° and 27.8° correspond to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively, and these peaks stem from the in-plane structural filling motifs of the conjugated aromatic system and characteristic interlayer superposition reflections [58]. The XRD patterns of CDs/PCNVs composites, even after CDs introduction on the PCNVs surface, strikingly resembled those of bulk g-C3N4, which implies that the original crystal structure of g-C3N4 remains intact post the introduction of CDs. However, the strength of the (100) peak in the CDs/PCNVs composites diminishes progressively with increasing CDs content, attributed to the porous nature of PCNVs causing periodic disruption of the in-plane structure [59]. Concurrently, the (002) diffraction peak slightly shifts as CDs increase, signifying the modulation of g-C3N4 layer distance by CDs and underscoring strong interfacial interactions between CDs and PCNVs [60]. To delve into the interactions within CD/PCNVs composites, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed (Fig. 3b). For PCNVs, vibrational bands at 807 cm−1 were ascribed to respiration modes of tris-s-triazine units [61], peaks in the range of 1246–1628 cm−1 corresponded to various aromatic C-N heterocyclic stretching modes [62], and broadband peaks in the range of about 3000–3700 cm−1 were attributed to N-H bonds [63]. Remarkably, all samples exhibited nearly identical characteristic peaks, affirming that the introduction of CDs did not alter the molecular structure of PCNVs. Additionally, no new peaks emerged in the FT-IR spectra, potentially attributed to the limited quantity of CDs and the overlap of a small number of CDs with the positions of PCNVs peaks [64]. Microstructural attributes, such as specific surface area, pore volume, and particle size distribution, are intricately linked to the quantity of reaction sites, playing a pivotal role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples [65]. Illustrated in Fig. 3c, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs exhibit a distinctive type IV classification with typical H3-type hysteresis loops, thereby confirming the presence of mesoporous structures [29]. Subsequently, the BET specific surface areas of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3 were computed as 89.52 and 202.83 m2/g, respectively. The markedly larger specific surface area of the synthesized CDs/PCNVs-3 compared to PCNVs of equivalent mass can be ascribed to the incorporation of CDs with smaller sizes onto the porous g-C3N4 vesicle nanoreactor structure. Simultaneously, the pore size distribution analysis revealed that both PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3 possessed numerous medium and large pores in the range of 2–100 nm (Fig. 3d). In contrast to PCNVs, CDs/PCNVs-3 exhibited a greater concentration of pores below 10 nm, resulting in a significantly higher specific surface area, enabling CDs/PCNVs-3 to produce more active sites, thereby augmenting its photocatalytic prowess [40]. In Fig. 3e, the surface of PCNVs exhibits hydrophilicity with a contact angle of 30.86° in the absence of CDs decoration. Upon the decoration of CDs on PCNVs, the contact angle remains nearly unchanged. Specifically, the contact angles for the CDs/PCNVs-1, CDs/PCNVs-3, and CDs/PCNVs-5 samples are measured at 31.63°, 32.89°, and 34.75°, respectively. This observed in contact angles indicates that the hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups, intrinsic to the CDs synthesized by this method, persist when modified on the PCNVs. These hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups contribute to maintaining the surface hydrophilicity of PCNVs and expediting electron transfer, thereby facilitating the rapid separation of photoinduced carriers [66]. Additional chemical insights and information regarding the chemical valence states of the as-prepared samples were extracted through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in the spectra presented in Fig. 3f and detailed in Table.S1, both PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3 samples comprised three elemental components, including C, N and O. Notably, the C/N atomic ratio experienced an increment from 75 % to 93 % following the introduction of CDs into CN, which serves as further confirmation of the successful modification of CDs/PCNVs composite. For a more in-depth understanding of the surface chemical structural properties, narrow-scan C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s XPS spectra of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3 samples were meticulously acquired. Specifically, as illustrated in Fig. 3g, the peaks in the C 1 s spectra near binding energies of 293.3, 287.7, and 284.8 eV were attributed to π-π*, C-N = H, and C-C, respectively [67]. The slight deviation in binding energies of C 1 s in CDs/PCNVs-3 compared to PCNVs could be attributed to the modification of surfaces by their unique porous structures [50]. Similarly, as indicated in Fig. 3h, all samples exhibited four peaks in close proximity to the binding energy in the N 1 s spectra, namely 404.1, 400.7, 399.7 and 398.3 eV, which were associated with the π excitation of the heptazine cycle, N-H, N-(C)3 and C-N = C, respectively [62]. Noteworthy is the observation that the N 1 s spectra of CDs/PCNVs-3 displayed minimal changes in binding energies, indicating that CDs have a subtle effect on the electronic structure of PCNVs. In the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3i), two discernible peaks at 532.8 and 531.6 eV for PCNVs were attributed to adsorbed oxygen species, such as water and oxygen [29]. Meanwhile, the O 1 s spectra of CDs/PCNVs-3 manifested two primary peaks at 533.0 and 531.4 eV, corresponding to O-H and C = O, respectively [68]. The introduction of CDs led to a pronounced increase in the intensity of the C = O peak for CDs/PCNVs-3, attributable to the influence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [32].

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity of the as-prepared CDs/PCNVs photocatalysts was assessed under AM 1.5 G irradiation condition using triethanolamine (TEOA) as electron donor and incorporating 1 wt% Pt as co-catalyst. In Fig. 4 a, it is evident that all samples facilitated the conversion of HER to H2 during the photocatalytic process. Notably, the CDs/PCNVs photocatalysts exhibited superior activities compared to pristine PCNVs, which was attributed to the outstanding light-absorbing ability of CDs, underscoring the favorable separation of photo-induced carriers, thereby promoting the reaction in the positive direction. Fig. 4b delineates the impact of varying CDs loadings in CDs/PCNVs on photocatalytic performance, as derived from the photocatalytic HER rate. Specifically, the photocatalytic HER activity and H2 yields of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs composites can be visualized in the following order: 3 % CDs/PCNVs > 5 % CDs/PCNVs > 7 % CDs/PCNVs > 1 % CDs/PCNVs > PCNVs, which the optimal photocatalytic H2 generation is observed at a 3 wt% CDs loading content. Beyond this concentration, the shading effect and aggregation of CDs result in the loss of the critical small size advantage, leading to decreased activity [34,69]. To unravel the carrier behavior of CDs/PCNVs photocatalysts, photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out. The transient photocurrent densities (Fig.S2) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests (Fig.S3) demonstrated that the CDs/PCNVs-3 photocatalysts possessed excellent charge transfer efficiency. In addition, for further understanding of the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of the prepared samples, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Fig.S4) and time-resolved transient photoluminescence (TRPL) quantification (Fig.S5) were employed to assess whether the CDs-loaded PCNVs suppressed charge-carrier complexation as well as effective separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) values of the optimal CDs/PCNVs-3 samples under monochromatic light irradiation are depicted in Fig. 4c. At 420, 650 and 800 nm, the AQE values of CDs/PCNVs-3 were 8.8 %, 2.0 % and 1.0 %, respectively. Besides, the CDs/PCNVs-3 sample with the advantage of simplicity of synthesis outperformed most of the previously reported CN-based photocatalytic systems in terms of hydrogen production rate (Fig. 4d and Table S2) when the experimental conditions with similar intensity of the sacrificial agent, co-catalyst, and light source were selected for comparison without controlling the temperature. In addition, the CDs/PCNVs-3 photocatalysts exhibited a slightly decreasing H2 evolution rate with prolonged cycling time in four photocatalytic cycling experiments (Fig. 4e). The XRD patterns of CDs/PCNVs-3 photocatalysts before and after photocatalysis (Fig. 4f) confirmed the stability of the crystal structure. Fig.S6 and Fig.S7 compare the TEM images and XPS spectra of CDs/PCNVs photocatalyst before and after photocatalysis, which further confirms that there is no obvious structural change of the used CDs/PCNVs, indicating that the prepared CDs/PCNVs materials possess good stability. In sequence, the photocatalytic production of H2 was carried out under real sunlight outside the laboratory, yielding an impressive 13.1 mmol g−1 for the CDs/PCNVs-3 sample (Fig. 4g and Fig.S8). When the photocatalyst was exposed to sunlight, bubbles began to overflow from the surface of the photocatalyst, and the decline in photocatalytic activity after 14:00 is attributed to diminishing daylight intensity and lower sacrificial agent concentration [70]. The outdoor experiments highlighted the efficient ambient H2 production capabilities of CDs/PCNVs-3 nanoreactor photocatalysts without additional energy input, offering practical insights for the application of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confinement insulation effect of the nanoreactors
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties play a pivotal role in influencing photocatalytic activity, and the optical absorption of CDs/PCNVs prepared in this study may change significantly with structural alteration and CDs introduction, whose alterations can be effectively characterized through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). In Fig. 6 a, the BCN sample adsorbed the visible light weakly with an absorption edge of around 480 nm, and all photocatalysts utilizing PCNVs as the substrate displayed robust UV–vis absorption. Notably, with increasing CDs content, the CDs/PCNVs composites exhibited higher absorption spectra in the 300–800 nm wavelength range compared to PCNVs, enhancing photons absorption for the generation of photo-generated charge carriers and facilitating photothermal H2 production [33]. Utilizing the Kubelk-Munk method [80,81], the band gap values of the synthesized PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs materials were determined to be 2.57 and 2.62 eV, respectively (Fig.S10). Complementing the Mott-Schottky plots in Fig.S11, the conduction band (CB) positions of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs were calculated to be −0.47 and −0.54 eV, respectively. Factoring in the optical bandgap and CB positions, the valence band (VB) values for PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs were calculated to be 2.10 and 2.11 eV, respectively. With the above analyses, the energy band structures of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs are schematically illustrated in Fig.S12. It is noteworthy that template-free self-assembly yielded PCNVs with a distinctive vesicle structure (Fig. 6b), effectively enhancing visible light absorption through increased contact area, propagation path intensity, and light scattering. Furthermore, the embedding of CDs on the surface of PCNVs, coupled with the domain-limiting effect of PCNVs, facilitated multiple refractions and reflections of light within the hollow structure. This intricate interplay improved photon utilization within internal CDs, thereby further enhancing the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs/PCNVs. The vesicle-like structured cavities, imparting a heat-collecting effect, constitute pivotal elements in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CDs/PCNVs photothermal nanoreactors. The heat generation and thermal insulation properties of the BCN, PCNNVs and CDs/PCNVs samples were systematically examined by subjecting them to AM 1.5 G Xenon lamp irradiation for 8 min and subsequently allowing natural cooling for another 8 min after turning off the light source. As depicted in Fig. 6c and d, a comparative analysis between BCN and PCNVs revealed that the vesicular nanoreactor structure exhibited faster heating and attained a higher final temperature under light irradiation conditions due to the reduction of the heat loss in the photocatalyst facilitated by the hollow structure. Notably, CDs/PCNVs-3 exhibited even faster heating and a higher final temperature, reaching 103.0 °C compared to PCNVs, and it can be inferred that the CDs are indeed effective in enhancing the heat generation and insulation properties of CDs/PCNVs samples. From the experimental point of view, it is evident that CDs indeed contribute to an increase in the temperature of the composites. Subsequent to turning off the light source for 8 min, the temperatures of BCN, PCNVs, and CDs/PCNVs-3 decreased to 30.9, 34.5, and 34.8 °C, respectively, underscoring the robust thermal insulation properties of the vesicle nanoreactor structures. Specifically, the spherical walls in the hollow vesicle structure serves as a thermal barrier, minimizing heat conduction both internally and externally, thereby reducing overall heat loss from the nanoreactor system [31]. Three sets of photothermal cycling curves were conducted on CDs/PCNVs-3 (Fig.S13), affirming the nanoreactor has exceptional photothermal conversion response and stability. Moreover, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 35.87 %) of CDs/PCNVs-3 was calculated based on the time constant (τS = 287.2 s) and the maximum steady-state temperature, as depicted in Fig. 6e, providing additional evidence that the CDs/PCNVs-3 nanoreactor rapidly and efficiently converts light into heat [82]. Transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) was employed to monitor the real-time relaxation kinetics of photogenerated charge carriers in CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors across ultrafast time scales [57]. TAS measurements were conducted with pump excitation at 343 nm, and the pseudo-color TAS spectra of PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3, tested in deionized water, are depicted in Fig. 6f and i. It is intuitively observed that the signals of all the samples were detected at around 850 nm. Notably, the signals of CDs/PCNVs-3 were markedly stronger than those of PCNVs, preliminarily indicating that CDs/PCNVs-3 exhibits more active carrier motion upon photoexcitation, with an extended electronic relaxation process [83]. In Fig. 6g and j, the TAS spectra on PCNVs and CDs/PCNVs-3 photocatalysts with varying delay times exhibited a consistent trend. Importantly, the excitation emission signals of CDs/PCNVs-3 (Fig. 6j) are similar to those of PCNVs (Fig. 6g), reflecting the complexation process of photo-induced carriers or bound excitons. The carrier relaxation time of CDs/PCNVs-3 was substantially extended in comparison to PCNVs, suggesting a higher likelihood for the photogenerated electrons to initiate the photocatalytic reaction [84]. For a more detailed characterization of differences in carrier activity, carrier kinetic curves at 800 nm were extracted and fitted (Fig. 6h and k). In PCNVs samples, the τ (127.2 ps) fractions indicated relatively fewer shallowly trapped electrons, which could be rapidly consumed before the surface-catalyzed reaction through fast hole on VB recombination and rapid depletion, posing challenges to the hydrogen production process [85]. Moreover, τ1 (37.2 ps) of CDs/PCNVs-3 were further reduced after the introduction of CDs, attributed to the additional proton reduction reaction between H+ photogenerated by CDs and the active shallow-trapped state electrons[86].

Based on the comprehensive characterization and experimental findings presented above, Fig. 7 illustrates the plausible mechanism of CDs/PCNVs composite photocatalyst for photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production. When exposed to simulated solar radiation, the CDs/PCNVs nanoreactor absorbs photons, generating photo-induced charges (e- and h+ ). These charges are injected into the CB and VB of the photocatalysts, and the generation of CDs on the surface of the PCNVs can efficiently capture surrounding photo-generated electrons due to the electron trapping effect of CDs [51], which makes it more likely to form an electron-rich region. This phenomenon facilitates rapid directional migration and volume phase separation of CN, enhancing the overall photocatalytic process. The photogenerated holes in the VB of CDs/PCNVs are consumed by TEOA, and a substantial number of photo-induced electrons retained on the CDs/PCNVs reduce protons for H2 formation. Moreover, the surfaces of the CDs offer abundant active sites where electrons bind to H+ through the Volmer-Heyrovsky process, contributing to H2 production. Kinetically, the photothermal effect provides additional energy to the photo-generated electrons and holes, expediting carrier complexation at the interface and achieving spatial separation of carriers. The cavity structure of PCNVs allows for multiple light bounces inside, promoting enhanced light absorption and utilization. Additionally, PCNVs act as confinement limiting insulation, mitigating rapid heat loss. Another aspect of the photothermal effect is the temperature elevation in the reaction system, effectively promoting the migration, adsorption, and reaction of reactant H2O molecules [30]. Overall, the synergistic coupling of the introduction of CDs and the photothermal effect play a pivotal role in significantly augmenting the photocatalytic activity of the CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors.

In conclusion, CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors, incorporating CDs within porous graphitic carbon nitride vesicles, were crafted using a straightforward one-step template-free thermal polymerization route for proficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production under simulated/real sunlight irradiation. Performance assessments revealed that the synthesized CDs/PCNVs-3 displayed remarkable photocatalytic activity, achieving an H2 production rate of 25.86 mmol h−1 g−1, approximately 2.2 times greater than that observed in untreated pure PCNVs. The enhanced hydrogen production performance can be attributed to several key factors: (i) CDs, functioning as both electron donor and acceptor, facilitate the swift migration of photoexcited charges, with CDs serving as effective photothermal materials for promoting photocatalytic hydrogen production; (ii) the expansive specific surface area of PCNVs nanoreactors augments the active sites of CDs, effectively enhancing the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic performance of the composites under photoexcitation; (iii) the porous cavity structure of PCNVs nanoreactors induces multiple reflections, thereby improving light utilization. Simultaneously, the structure exhibits a photothermal-constrained heat-collecting effect, preventing rapid heat dissipation and achieving thermal insulation, thereby greatly facilitating photocatalytic hydrogen evolution; (iv) CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors exhibit exceptional hydrophilicity. Within the spatial photothermal effect, water molecules surrounding the high-temperature CDs/PCNVs nanoreactors undergo accelerated movement, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic reaction rate through the promotion of localized mass transfer kinetics. This work provides valuable insights and references for the design and development of efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalysts. In addition, the simple and economically feasible synthesis of the CDs demonstrates the practicality and promise of CDs-based composite photocatalysts.
